name: Psychiatric morbidity in gender-referred adolescents (Finland register study)
intervention: Referral to specialised gender identity services
comparator: Matched population controls (4 male + 4 female per case)
summary: In a Finnish nationwide register cohort of all under-23 gender-referred individuals in 1996–2019 (2,083 cases vs 16,643 birth-year- and municipality-matched controls), gender-referred adolescents needed specialist-level psychiatric treatment about 3 times more often than controls before referral and about 4 times more often ≥2 years after. The elevated need was seen in every gender-direction and gender-reassignment subgroup, and the study reports that psychiatric needs did not subside after medical gender reassignment.
description: A register-based matched cohort (Ruuska 2026, Acta Paediatrica) following all individuals under age 23 who contacted Finland's two centralised gender identity clinics in 1996–2019, with national Care Register follow-up to June 2022 (mean 5.49 years). The exposure is gender-identity-service referral; the outcome is need for specialist-level psychiatric treatment (accepted specialist referrals, a marker of severe psychiatric morbidity), recorded before the index contact and ≥2 years after it. All effects here are computed live from the study's raw counts (Tables 1 and 3).
interpretation: This is observational, so associations are not causal—the study itself stresses confounding. The before-referral gap (≈3×) shows the morbidity largely predates any gender-service contact. The most striking pattern is selection by indication: before medical gender reassignment the GR+ subgroups had the LOWEST psychiatric-treatment rates (toward-female 9.8%, toward-male 21.6%—at or below controls), because severe psychiatric morbidity is a relative contraindication to medical GR; ≥2 years later those same groups had risen to 60.7% and 54.5%. So the post-GR levels reflect both a healthier-at-baseline group and a large increase over time, and cannot be read as medical GR causing—or preventing—morbidity. After adjusting for prior psychiatric treatment, all gender-referred groups still had ~3-fold higher need vs female controls and ~5-fold vs male controls (Tables 4–5). Need was higher in the post-2010 cohort than the 1996–2010 cohort (pre-referral need roughly doubled). The authors' conclusion: severe psychiatric morbidity is common in gender-referred adolescents and does not subside after medical gender reassignment, so psychiatric needs must be assessed and treated throughout.
methodologyNotes: Each outcome is entered as the study's raw 2×2 counts and the risk ratio, CI, and number-needed (here number-needed-to-be-exposed for one extra case) are computed live—nothing hardcoded. The two overall outcomes use Table 1 (GD 2,083 vs controls 16,643). The four subgroup outcomes use Table 3 and compare each gender-direction × GR-status group to its same registered-sex control group (toward-female groups are registered males, compared with control males; toward-male groups are registered females, compared with control females), matching the study's matched-cohort design; these are unadjusted comparisons, and the study's covariate-adjusted hazard ratios (Tables 4–5) are described in the interpretation rather than re-derived. Subgroups are kept as separate outcomes, never pooled, because they share overlapping control groups. The before→after trajectory within the GR+ groups is paired (same individuals) and confounded by indication, so it is described in prose, not encoded as an effect. Status is set to harmful on each outcome to reflect the direction of the association (the gender-referred group has significantly higher psychiatric morbidity); because this is a single—though very large (n=18,726)—register study, the automatic k<3 'limited' label is overridden. 'Harmful' denotes the direction of the association, not a causal claim about referral.

outcomes.label: Specialist psychiatric treatment ≥2 years after referral
outcomes.description: Need for specialist-level psychiatric treatment continuing ≥2 years after the index contact, gender-referred individuals vs all matched controls (Table 1).

outcomes.label: Specialist psychiatric treatment before referral
outcomes.description: Need for specialist-level psychiatric treatment before the index contact, gender-referred individuals vs all matched controls (Table 1).

outcomes.label: After referral — toward female, no medical GR (vs control males)
outcomes.description: Registered males seeking transition to female who did not undergo medical gender reassignment, vs control males, ≥2 years after referral (Table 3).

outcomes.label: After referral — toward male, no medical GR (vs control females)
outcomes.description: Registered females seeking transition to male who did not undergo medical gender reassignment, vs control females, ≥2 years after referral (Table 3).

outcomes.label: After referral — toward female, after feminising GR (vs control males)
outcomes.description: Registered males who underwent feminising medical gender reassignment, vs control males, ≥2 years after referral (Table 3). Note the before→after rise from 9.8% to 60.7% described in the interpretation.

outcomes.label: After referral — toward male, after masculinising GR (vs control females)
outcomes.description: Registered females who underwent masculinising medical gender reassignment, vs control females, ≥2 years after referral (Table 3). Note the before→after rise from 21.6% to 54.5% described in the interpretation.

studies.author: Ruuska
studies.citation: Ruuska SM, Tuisku K, Holttinen T, Kaltiala R. Psychiatric morbidity among adolescents and young adults who contacted specialised gender identity services in Finland in 1996–2019: a register study. Acta Paediatr. 2026;115(8):1545-1553.
studies.population: Finnish nationwide register cohort, all gender-referred individuals under age 23, 1996–2019
studies.endpointDefinition: Need for specialist-level psychiatric treatment (accepted specialist referral) continuing ≥2 years after the index contact.
studies.notes: Mean follow-up 5.49 years. Adjusted HRs vs controls were ~3-fold (female reference) to ~5-fold (male reference) after accounting for prior psychiatric treatment.

studies.author: Ruuska
studies.citation: Ruuska SM, Tuisku K, Holttinen T, Kaltiala R. Psychiatric morbidity among adolescents and young adults who contacted specialised gender identity services in Finland in 1996–2019: a register study. Acta Paediatr. 2026;115(8):1545-1553.
studies.population: Finnish nationwide register cohort, all gender-referred individuals under age 23, 1996–2019
studies.endpointDefinition: Need for specialist-level psychiatric treatment before the index contact.
studies.notes: Morbidity largely predates gender-service contact. The pre-referral gap was larger in the 2011–2019 cohort (47.9% vs 15.3%) than 1996–2010 (23.7% vs 11.8%).

studies.author: Ruuska
studies.citation: Ruuska SM, Tuisku K, Holttinen T, Kaltiala R. Psychiatric morbidity among adolescents and young adults who contacted specialised gender identity services in Finland in 1996–2019: a register study. Acta Paediatr. 2026;115(8):1545-1553.
studies.population: Registered males, toward female, no medical GR (n=318) vs control males (n=8,325)
studies.endpointDefinition: Specialist psychiatric treatment ≥2 years after referral.

studies.citation: Ruuska SM, Tuisku K, Holttinen T, Kaltiala R. Psychiatric morbidity among adolescents and young adults who contacted specialised gender identity services in Finland in 1996–2019: a register study. Acta Paediatr. 2026;115(8):1545-1553.
studies.population: Registered females, toward male, no medical GR (n=969) vs control females (n=8,318)
studies.endpointDefinition: Specialist psychiatric treatment ≥2 years after referral.

studies.population: Registered males who underwent feminising GR (n=163) vs control males (n=8,325)
studies.endpointDefinition: Specialist psychiatric treatment ≥2 years after referral.
studies.notes: This group's need was 9.8% before GR (at/below controls) and 60.7% ≥2 years after.

studies.endpointDefinition: Specialist psychiatric treatment ≥2 years after referral.
studies.notes: This group's need was 21.6% before GR and 54.5% ≥2 years after.
